skill.name: agent-workflow
skill.description: Guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through a structured multi-agent development workflow with role-based phases and subagent coordination.
skill.summary: Coordinate requirements, design, implementation, verification, and reflection with role-based AI subagents.
skill.author: Clouder0

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged Markdown code fences and inline backticks as Ruby shell execution, but the file only documents Task tool examples and agent names. Static high findings for weak cryptography are false positives from prose terms such as description, architecture, and Architect; no cryptographic code, shell execution, network access, or prompt injection was found.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Task Examples Flagged as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The flagged external command locations are Markdown fenced examples and inline code labels for the Task tool. They do not execute Ruby, shell commands, or user-controlled command strings.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines are inside Markdown examples or tables, not executable source files. The skill declares only Read, Task, and Glob tools and contains no shell invocation syntax outside documentation.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Keywords in Prose
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography hits are word collisions in plain English workflow text, including description, architecture, Architect, and Roundtable. No hash function, cipher API, or cryptographic operation is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact lines contain human-readable role and phase labels, not code. There is no evidence of cryptographic libraries, algorithms, keys, encryption, or digest generation.

content.value_statement: Complex development tasks can lose clarity when one assistant handles every phase. This skill defines a role-based workflow for planning, implementation, verification, and reflection.
actual_capabilities: Defines five workflow phases for requirements, architecture, implementation, verification, and reflection.
Lists Tier-1 coordinator roles and Tier-2 worker roles for agent delegation.
Shows Task tool patterns for custom dotagent subagents and Claude Code built-in agents.
Maps each subagent type to an intended model and responsibility.
References workflow state files used to track phase and progress.
limitations: Provides orchestration guidance only; it does not include the referenced agent definition files.
Does not implement automated workflow state updates or validation checks.
Requires an environment that supports the Task tool and matching subagent names.
Does not define project-specific acceptance criteria or testing commands.

use_cases.title: Plan a Feature Across Roles
use_cases.description: Use role-based phases to move a feature from requirements through design before assigning implementation work.

use_cases.title: Delegate Codebase Exploration
use_cases.description: Spawn explorer or planning agents to gather focused context before making a code change.

use_cases.title: Verify Multi-Step Changes
use_cases.description: Use verifier and tester roles to review behavior, run checks, and document remaining risks.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a new development request
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the agent-workflow skill to clarify this development task, identify the current phase, and recommend the next role to involve.

prompt_templates.title: Explore Before Editing
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the explorer role to find the files, tests, and patterns related to this change. Summarize findings and unknowns before editing.

prompt_templates.title: Coordinate Implementation
prompt_templates.scenario: Manage a scoped coding task with subagents
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the workflow phases to split this task into implementation units, assign appropriate roles, and define verification steps for each unit.

prompt_templates.title: Run Full Review Cycle
prompt_templates.prompt: Orchestrate requirements, architecture, implementation, QA, and reflection for this project. Record decisions, risks, test coverage, and follow-up work.

output_examples.input: A developer asks to add a new authentication setting to an existing service.
output_examples.output: The workflow first assigns requirements gathering, then architecture review, then focused implementation, followed by verifier and tester review.

output_examples.input: A repository has an unclear failure and the user wants a safe fix.
output_examples.output: The skill directs exploration first, records likely ownership areas, and delays implementation until the affected files and tests are understood.

output_examples.input: A large refactor needs multiple independent checks before release.
output_examples.output: The workflow separates implementation coordination from QA, then captures risks and lessons in the reflection phase.

best_practices: Start with requirements and acceptance criteria before spawning implementation agents.
Use focused subagents for bounded research, implementation, verification, and testing tasks.
Record phase decisions and unresolved risks so later agents inherit clear context.
anti_patterns: Do not spawn multiple agents before defining task boundaries and expected outputs.
Do not skip verification when the implementation touches shared behavior or user workflows.
Do not treat the workflow as a substitute for project-specific tests and review standards.

faq.question: What does this skill help coordinate?
faq.answer: It coordinates development work across requirements, architecture, implementation, verification, and reflection phases.

faq.question: Does this skill execute code or commands?
faq.answer: No. The skill is a Markdown workflow guide and does not include executable scripts.

faq.question: Which tools does it expect?
faq.answer: It expects Read, Task, and Glob support, with Task used to delegate work to subagents.

faq.question: Can it work without custom dotagent files?
faq.answer: It can still describe the workflow, but custom subagent references require matching agent definitions in the environment.

faq.question: Is it only for Claude Code?
faq.answer: No. The report lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code support, but some agent names are Claude Code specific.

faq.question: How should teams use it safely?
faq.answer: Teams should keep tasks bounded, review delegated outputs, and run normal tests before accepting implementation changes.
